function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Controlled-company status affects governance requirements but not necessarily economic rights. Distinguish between governance control and economic ownership using the filing’s dual-class structure and board-election mechanics.

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's controlled-company status, dual-class structure, and board-election mechanics. I need to retrieve information from the SpaceX S-1 filing to answer this question about governance control vs. economic ownership.

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieving relevant information about:
1. Dual-class structure
2. Board-election mechanics
3. Controlled-company status
4. Governance control vs. economic ownership

Let me parse the filing first.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about the dual-class structure, board-election mechanics, and controlled-company status from the filing.

arguments.prompt: What is the dual-class stock structure in {{spacex_s1}}? Describe the classes of common stock, voting rights per share, and how voting power is allocated between classes.
Describe the controlled-company status and governance exemptions in {{spacex_s1}}. What NYSE or Nasdaq listing rules is the company exempt from? What is the board composition requirement?

Describe the board of directors election mechanics in {{spacex_s1}}. How are directors elected? Is it plurality or majority voting? What is the classified/staggered board structure? How many directors are there?

results.content:
# Dual-Class Stock Structure in SpaceX's IPO

## Classes of Common Stock

Based on the prospectus excerpts, SpaceX has **four classes of common stock** authorized, all with par value of $0.001 per share:

| Class | Authorized Shares | Voting Rights | Outstanding Post-IPO |
|-------|------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Class A** | 36,132,150,000 | 1 vote per share | Being offered in IPO |
| **Class B** | 6,125,000,000 | 10 votes per share | Outstanding (concentrated ownership) |
| **Class C** | 10,000,000,000 | No voting rights | None issued/outstanding |
| **Class D** | (authorized) | To be established when issued | None issued/outstanding |

Additionally, **2,400,000,000 shares of preferred stock** are authorized but none are outstanding.

## Voting Rights Per Share

- **Class A**: One vote per share
- **Class B**: Ten votes per share
- **Class C**: No voting rights (except as required by law)
- **Class D**: Voting rights to be established by the Board if/when issued

## Allocation of Voting Power

### General Voting (Single Class)
Class A and Class B shareholders **vote together as a single class** on all matters submitted to shareholders, except where Class B has special class voting rights.

### Board Election (Split Voting)
- **Class B shareholders**, voting separately as a class, elect **51% of the total authorized directors** (rounded up to the nearest whole number)—these are the "Class B Directors"
- **Class A and Class B together**, voting as a single class, elect the remaining directors—the "Common Stock Directors"

### Special Class B Protective Provisions
Class B shareholders, voting separately as a class, have exclusive approval rights over:

1. **Removal of Elon Musk** from his board and leadership roles (CEO and Chairman)
2. **Removal of Class B Directors** (with or without cause)
3. **Filling vacancies** among Class B Directors
4. **Charter amendments** affecting Class B rights, powers, preferences, or privileges
5. **Certain combinations, mergers, or sales** as described in the charter

### Conversion Mechanics
- Each Class B share is **convertible at holder's option** into one Class A share at any time
- **Automatic conversion** of Class B to Class A occurs upon any transfer (whether or not for value), except for "Permitted Transfers" (certain inter-family transfers and transfers to qualifying entities)
- This mechanism means that over time, as Class B shares are sold/transferred, they convert to lower-vote Class A, **increasing the relative voting power** of remaining Class B holders

## Concentration of Control

The structure is designed to consolidate control with Elon Musk:

- Musk beneficially owns **approximately 93.6% of Class B common stock** (pre-offering)
- This translates to **approximately 85.1% of combined voting power** (pre-offering)
- Post-IPO, Musk will hold a **majority of the voting power** of common stock
- As a result, Musk can:
  - Elect **all members of the board** (Class B Directors through Class B ownership; Common Stock Directors through combined voting power)
  - Control the outcome of **all matters requiring shareholder approval**
  - Only be removed from his CEO/Chairman positions by a **Class B shareholder vote**

## "Controlled Company" Status

Due to this concentration, SpaceX will qualify as a **"controlled company"** under Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing rules, allowing it to rely on exemptions from certain corporate governance requirements, including:
- Not requiring a majority of independent directors on the board
- Not requiring independent compensation and nominating committees

However, the company remains subject to the requirement for an **audit committee composed entirely of independent directors**.

## Anti-Takeover Considerations

The dual-class structure, combined with Texas anti-takeover statutes (Section 21.606 of the TBOC), creates significant barriers to unsolicited acquisitions or changes in control, as public Class A shareholders have **limited or precluded ability** to influence corporate matters or board elections.

# Controlled-Company Status and Governance Exemptions

## Basis for Controlled-Company Status

Under the listing rules of **Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas**, a company of which more than 50% of the voting power with respect to director elections is held by another person or group of persons acting together is a "controlled company." Following the IPO, Elon Musk will beneficially own shares of Class A and Class B common stock representing **greater than 50% of the voting power** of the company's common stock with respect to director elections. Additionally, under the charter, holders of Class B common stock (voting separately as a class) are entitled to elect **51% of the total number of authorized directors** (rounded up to the nearest whole number). Because Mr. Musk will hold a majority of the Class B common stock, he will control the election of the Class B Directors and, for so long as he holds a majority of the combined voting power, the remainder of the board as well.

---

## Specific Nasdaq / Nasdaq Texas Listing Rules the Company Is Exempt From

As a controlled company, SpaceX may elect **not to comply** with the following Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas corporate governance requirements:

| # | Exempted Requirement |
|---|---|
| 1 | **Majority-independent board** — A majority of the company's board of directors must consist of independent directors as defined under the listing rules. |
| 2 | **Independent nominating committee** — Director nominees must be selected or recommended for the board's selection by a nominating committee composed **entirely of independent directors**, with a written charter addressing the nominations process. |
| 3 | **Independent compensation committee** — The compensation committee must be composed **entirely of independent directors**, with a written charter addressing the committee's purpose and responsibilities. |
| 4 | **Annual committee performance evaluations** — Annual performance evaluations of the compensation and nominating committees must be conducted. |

> **Note:** The text references only **Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas** listing rules — not NYSE. The company has applied to list its Class A common stock on Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas under the symbol **"SPCX."**

### What Remains Required

The company explicitly states that, even as a controlled company, it **remains subject to the rule requiring an audit committee composed entirely of independent directors.** This requirement is **not** exempted.

---

## Board Composition Requirement (and How the Company Will Operate Without It)

### The Exempted Standard

The standard Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas requirement from which the company is exempt is that **a majority of the board of directors consist of independent directors.** A controlled company need not meet this threshold.

### Actual Post-IPO Board Composition

The company's board will consist of **eight directors**, divided into two categories under the charter:

| Director Class | Elected By | Initial Members |
|---|---|---|
| **Class B Directors** (51% of authorized directors, rounded up) | Class B common stock, voting separately as a class | Elon Musk, Gwynne Shotwell, Antonio J. Gracias, Donald Harrison, Luke Nosek |
| **Common Stock Directors** (remaining members) | Class A and Class B common stock, voting together as a single class | Ira Ehrenpreis, Randy Glein, Steve Jurvetson |

The board is subject to **annual elections**, with each director holding office until the next annual meeting and until a successor is duly elected and qualified, or until earlier death, resignation, or removal.

### Independence Determinations

The board has determined that **five of the eight directors** are independent within the meaning of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas listing standards:

- Ira Ehrenpreis
- Randy Glein
- Donald Harrison
- Steve Jurvetson
- Luke Nosek

This means **five of eight** directors (a majority) are independent — though the company is not *required* to maintain this majority so long as it remains a controlled company.

---

## Committee Structure Under the Exemptions

### Compensation and Nominating Committee

- **Expected members:** Ira Ehrenpreis (chair), Antonio J. Gracias, and Luke Nosek.
- The company **will rely on the controlled-company exemption** from the requirement that this committee be composed entirely of independent directors with a charter addressing all applicable Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas requirements.
- Ira Ehrenpreis and Luke Nosek each qualify as independent (including heightened independence standards for compensation committee members) and as "non-employee directors" under Rule 16b-3. Antonio J. Gracias's independence status is not affirmatively stated in the excerpt.
- The committee's responsibilities include overseeing compensation philosophy, reviewing/approving executive compensation, recommending director candidates, and performing other board-assigned functions.

### Audit Committee

- **Expected members:** Randy Glein (chair) and Steve Jurvetson, with a third member to be identified within the applicable one-year transition period under Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas rules.
- Randy Glein is expected to qualify as an "audit committee financial expert" under SEC rules implementing Section 407 of Sarbanes-Oxley.
- Both qualify as independent for purposes of Rule 10A-3 of the Exchange Act and Nasdaq/Nasdaq Texas listing standards.
- The audit committee is **not exempt** from independence requirements.

---

## Historical Compensation Practices

During the last completed fiscal year, the company was not publicly traded and had no compensation committee. The **full board** determined and made all decisions regarding Mr. Musk's compensation. For other executive officers, **Mr. Musk had primary responsibility** for compensation-related decisions, though all equity awards were approved by the board.

---

## Shareholder Protection Warning

The company expressly warns that, as a result of utilizing these exemptions, **Class A shareholders will not have the same protections** afforded to shareholders of companies subject to all Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas corporate governance requirements. The company may elect to take advantage of certain other exemptions in the future for as long as it remains a "controlled company."

If the company **ceases to be a controlled company** while its shares remain listed on Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas, it will be required to comply with all applicable governance requirements within the applicable transition periods.

Based on the provided corporate governance documents, here is a breakdown of the board of directors election mechanics:

**How many directors are there?**
Upon the consummation of the offering, the board will consist of **eight directors**. However, subject to the terms of the company's charter and bylaws, the board has the authority to determine the exact number of directors from time to time. 

**How are directors elected?**
Directors are elected through a dual-class voting structure, and the board is subject to **annual elections**. Each director holds office until the next annual meeting of shareholders and until a successor is duly elected and qualified, or until their earlier death, resignation, or removal. The election mechanics are structured as follows:
*   **Class B Directors:** Holders of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, have the right to elect 51% of the total number of authorized directors (rounded up to the nearest whole number). For an eight-member board, this equates to 5 directors. Class B shares carry 10 votes per share.
*   **Common Stock Directors:** Holders of all classes of voting common stock (Class A and Class B), voting together as a single class, elect the remaining members of the board (3 directors). Class A shares carry 1 vote per share.
*   **No Cumulative Voting:** The charter explicitly states that it will not permit cumulative voting in the election of directors.

*Initial Board Composition:* Upon completion of the offering, Elon Musk, Gwynne Shotwell, Antonio J. Gracias, Donald Harrison, and Luke Nosek will serve as the initial Class B Directors. Ira Ehrenpreis, Randy Glein, and Steve Jurvetson will serve as the initial Common Stock Directors.

**Is it plurality or majority voting?**
The provided text **does not specify** whether the standard for electing directors is plurality or majority voting. It only details the class voting structure for electing directors. (Note: The text does mention "majority" voting requirements, but specifically in the context of *removing* directors or filling vacancies, not the initial election standard). 

**What is the classified/staggered board structure?**
The company does **not** have a classified (or staggered) board structure. The text explicitly states that the "board will be subject to annual elections," meaning all directors stand for election each year rather than having multi-year staggered terms.

steps.reasoning_content: I have a good understanding of the dual-class structure and board-election mechanics. Now let me also retrieve information about economic ownership - specifically how much of the economic ownership Elon Musk holds vs. voting power. This is the key distinction the question is asking about.

arguments.prompt: What is Elon Musk's economic ownership percentage of SpaceX common stock in {{spacex_s1}}? How many Class A and Class B shares does he beneficially own? What percentage of total economic ownership does he hold versus voting power?

What are the conversion mechanics for Class B common stock in {{spacex_s1}}? When does Class B automatically convert to Class A? What are Permitted Transfers? How does this affect the sunset of dual-class structure?

results.content:
Based on the provided table and text, here is the breakdown of Elon Musk's ownership in SpaceX:

**1. Shares Beneficially Owned**
Before the offering, Elon Musk beneficially owns:
*   **Class A common stock:** 849,494,440 shares
*   **Class B common stock:** 5,569,053,075 shares

**2. Economic Ownership Percentage**
The table details his economic ownership by class:
*   **Class A common stock:** 12.3%
*   **Class B common stock:** 93.6%

*Note on Total Economic Ownership:* While the table does not provide a single combined "total economic ownership" percentage, it can be calculated using the share counts provided in the text. With 6,932,508,000 Class A shares and 5,602,790,410 Class B shares outstanding, Musk's total economic ownership across both classes of common stock is approximately **51.2%**. 

**3. Economic Ownership vs. Voting Power**
*   **Total Economic Ownership:** ~51.2% (calculated based on total outstanding shares)
*   **Combined Voting Power:** **85.1%** (as explicitly stated in the table)

This disparity between his ~51% economic ownership and 85.1% voting power is due to SpaceX's dual-class stock structure. Each share of Class A common stock carries 1 vote, while each share of Class B common stock carries 10 votes. Because Musk holds the vast majority (93.6%) of the high-vote Class B shares, he exercises controlling voting power over the company despite owning roughly half of the total economic equity.

# Class B Common Stock Conversion Mechanics

## 1. Voluntary Conversion (Holder Option)

Each share of Class B common stock is **convertible at any time at the option of the holder** into **one share** of Class A common stock, on a **1:1 ratio**. This is a unilateral right of the holder and requires no corporate action or approval.

## 2. Automatic Conversion Upon Transfer

The primary automatic conversion trigger is a **"Transfer"** of the Class B share. The charter defines "Transfer" broadly to include:

> "any sale, assignment, encumbrance, transfer, conveyance, hypothecation, pledge, gift, or other transfer or disposition of any kind of such share of Class B common stock or any legal or beneficial interest in such share, **whether or not for value** and **whether voluntary or involuntary or by operation of law**, including, without limitation, the transfer of, or entering into a binding agreement with respect to, **voting control over such share by proxy or otherwise**."

Key features of this trigger:
- It applies to **both legal and beneficial interests** in the share.
- It captures **non-value transfers** (e.g., gifts) and **involuntary transfers** (e.g., by operation of law).
- It expressly includes **transfers of voting control by proxy or binding agreement**, preventing holders from delegating voting power to third parties while retaining nominal ownership to preserve the 10-vote multiplier.
- The conversion is **automatic** — it occurs by operation of the charter, not by board discretion.

## 3. Permitted Transfers (Exceptions to Automatic Conversion)

The charter carves out **"Permitted Transfers"** that do **not** trigger automatic conversion. These fall into five categories:

| Category | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **(i)** | Transfers **to and from the registered holders** of Class B common stock (i.e., transfers among existing Class B holders of record). |
| **(ii)** | Transfers by natural persons who transferred Class B shares or equity awards (including options or warrants exercisable/convertible into Class B) to certain **"permitted entities"** as defined in the charter. |
| **(iii)** | Transfers to **family members** of shareholders described in categories (i) and (ii). |
| **(iv)** | Transfers to certain **trusts, general partnerships, limited partnerships, LLCs, corporations, or other entities** owned by certain "qualified shareholders" (as defined in the charter), including certain permitted non-for-profits. |
| **(v)** | Transfers to **bona fide trusts for the benefit of charitable organizations** (contributions deductible for federal income, estate, gift, and GST tax purposes), to **certain retirement accounts**, and for **certain estate or succession planning purposes**. |

"Permitted Transferees" are the recipients of Class B shares in a Transfer that qualifies as a Permitted Transfer. The design of these exceptions ensures that the high-vote shares remain within a controlled circle of the original holders, their families, and their estate-planning vehicles.

## 4. Impact on the Sunset of the Dual-Class Structure

### No Time-Based Sunset

The charter contains **no automatic time-based sunset** provision. Unlike some dual-class structures that convert all high-vote shares to low-vote shares after a fixed number of years or upon the founder's death, this structure **persists indefinitely** so long as Class B shares remain outstanding and are not transferred outside the Permitted Transfer framework.

### Gradual Erosion Through Transfers

The conversion-on-transfer mechanic functions as a **transfer-based sunset**:

> "The conversion of Class B common stock to Class A common stock will have the effect, over time, of **increasing the relative voting power of those holders of Class B common stock who retain their shares**."

This means:
- Each transfer of Class B shares (outside Permitted Transfers) permanently removes those shares from the high-vote class.
- The remaining Class B holders see their **relative voting power increase** because the total number of Class B shares declines while their individual holdings remain unchanged.
- The dual-class structure does not "sunset" in the conventional sense; rather, it **concentrates** over time into fewer hands.

### Control Concentration Risk

The prospectus explicitly warns of a control-shift risk:

> "If Mr. Musk were to sell, transfer or otherwise dispose of a sufficient number of his shares of Class B common stock such that he no longer holds a majority of the outstanding shares of Class B common stock, **another holder or group of holders of Class B common stock could obtain the ability to elect and remove a majority of our board** and thereby effectively control the Company."

This is a critical structural feature: because Class B holders (voting separately as a class) elect **51% of the authorized directors** for so long as any Class B shares remain outstanding, control of the board is determined by **whoever holds a majority of the outstanding Class B shares** — not by the overall shareholder base. If Mr. Musk's Class B holdings fall below 50% of outstanding Class B shares (through transfers that convert to Class A), control of the board could shift to another Class B holder or coalition, even if Mr. Musk remains the largest economic shareholder.

### No Separate Class Votes Under Texas Law

The charter opts out of separate class voting rights that would otherwise be required under the Texas Business Organizations Code (TBOC) — including for increases/decreases in authorized shares, exchanges, reclassifications, or cancellations — **except** for:
1. Amendments to the charter that change the rights, powers, preferences, or privileges of Class B common stock (including Class B Director provisions); and
2. Certain combinations, mergers, or sales described in the charter.

This opt-out further entrenches the Class B holders' control by preventing Class A holders from using statutory class vote protections to block corporate actions.

## Summary

| Mechanic | Detail |
|----------|--------|
| **Voluntary conversion** | At holder's option, anytime, 1:1 into Class A |
| **Automatic conversion trigger** | Any "Transfer" (broadly defined) of the Class B share or any legal/beneficial interest therein, whether or not for value, voluntary or involuntary |
| **Permitted Transfers (exceptions)** | Transfers among registered Class B holders; to permitted entities; to family members; to qualified-shareholder-owned entities/trusts; to charitable trusts, retirement accounts, and estate-planning vehicles |
| **Time-based sunset** | **None** — no automatic expiration |
| **Transfer-based sunset** | Gradual erosion of Class B count through non-permitted transfers; remaining holders' relative voting power increases |
| **Board control** | Class B holders (as a class) elect 51% of directors so long as any Class B shares are outstanding; control follows majority of outstanding Class B shares |
| **Control-shift risk** | If Mr. Musk's Class B holdings fall below majority of outstanding Class B, another holder/coalition could gain board control |

arguments.prompt: What is the total number of Class A and Class B common stock shares outstanding in {{spacex_s1}}? What is the total economic ownership percentage of Elon Musk across both classes combined? Provide the exact share counts from the beneficial ownership table.

In {{spacex_s1}}, what is the difference between voting power and economic ownership for Elon Musk? How does the dual-class structure create a wedge between economic interest and voting control? What percentage of voting power does Musk hold post-IPO?

results.content:
# Total Shares Outstanding and Elon Musk's Economic Ownership

## Total Shares Outstanding (as stated in the table's basis)

| Class | Shares Outstanding |
|-------|-------------------|
| Class A common stock | 6,932,508,000 |
| Class B common stock | 5,602,790,410 |
| **Total combined** | **12,535,298,410** |

## Elon Musk's Beneficial Ownership (from the table)

| Class | Shares | Table % |
|-------|--------|---------|
| Class A common stock | 849,494,440 | 12.3% |
| Class B common stock | 5,569,053,075 | 93.6% |
| **Total combined** | **6,418,547,515** | — |

## Total Economic Ownership Percentage

$$\frac{6,418,547,515}{12,535,298,410} \approx \textbf{51.2\%}$$

Elon Musk holds approximately **51.2% of the total economic ownership** across both classes combined, while commanding **85.1% of the combined voting power** (as shown in the table) — the disparity reflecting the 10-votes-per-share structure of Class B stock.

# Voting Power vs. Economic Ownership for Elon Musk

## 1. The Difference Between Voting Power and Economic Ownership

**Economic ownership** represents Musk's claim on the company's assets, earnings, and dividends — essentially his percentage of total shares outstanding (Class A + Class B combined). **Voting power** represents his ability to control corporate decisions through votes.

Based on the pre-offering figures in the table, we can derive the wedge:

| Metric | Calculation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A shares owned** | 849,494,440 (12.3% of Class A) | — |
| **Class B shares owned** | 5,569,053,075 (93.6% of Class B) | — |
| **Total shares (economic)** | ~6,418,547,515 of ~12.86 billion | **~50% economic ownership** |
| **Total votes (Class A × 1 + Class B × 10)** | ~56.5 billion of ~66.4 billion | **~85.1% voting power** |

Musk holds roughly **half the economic interest** but **~85% of the voting power** — a wedge of approximately **35 percentage points**.

## 2. How the Dual-Class Structure Creates the Wedge

The wedge is engineered through three mechanisms:

- **10:1 voting ratio**: Each Class B share carries 10 votes; each Class A share carries 1 vote. This means Class B shares are "over-weighted" in voting relative to their economic stake.

- **Concentrated Class B ownership**: Musk holds 93.6% of all Class B shares, meaning he captures nearly the entirety of the 10x voting multiplier. Public investors, by contrast, can only purchase Class A shares (1 vote each) in the IPO.

- **IPO dilution asymmetry**: The offering issues only Class A shares, which dilutes Musk's economic ownership proportionally but barely touches his voting power — because the new shares each carry only 1 vote against his existing 55.7 billion Class B votes.

As the filing states: *"This concentration of voting power will limit or preclude the ability of holders of our Class A common stock, including purchasers of Class A common stock in this offering, to influence corporate matters and the election of our directors."*

## 3. Post-IPO Voting Power Percentage

The S-1 leaves the exact post-IPO figure blank (denoted by `________%`), as the offering price and share count are not yet finalized. However, the filing repeatedly confirms:

- Musk will hold **"a majority of the voting power of the common stock"** post-IPO
- He will hold **"greater than 50% of the voting power"** with respect to director elections
- The company will qualify as a **"controlled company"** under Nasdaq rules (which requires >50% voting power held by a single person)

Given the pre-offering baseline of **85.1%** and that IPO-issued Class A shares (1 vote each) will only modestly dilute his ~56.5 billion votes, his post-IPO voting power will remain **well above 50%**, likely in the **high 70s to low 80s percent range** depending on offering size — while his economic ownership will drop below 50%.

This structural wedge means public shareholders will bear a significant share of economic risk and reward while having essentially no ability to influence board composition, executive removal, or strategic direction.

   - Class A: 1 vote per share (publicly offered)
   - Class B: 10 votes per share (concentrated with Musk)

2. **Board-election mechanics**:
   - 8-member board, annually elected (not staggered)

arguments.final_result:
## Governance Control vs. Economic Ownership in SpaceX's Dual-Class Structure

The SpaceX S-1 illustrates a sharp divergence between **governance control** (the ability to direct corporate action through votes and board elections) and **economic ownership** (the claim on assets, earnings, and dividends). The filing engineers this wedge through three interlocking mechanisms: a multi-class capital structure, class-segregated board-election mechanics, and controlled-company exemptions.

---

### 1. The Dual-Class Capital Structure

SpaceX's charter authorizes four classes of common stock (par value $0.001):

| Class | Votes/Share | Authorized Shares | Economic Role |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Class A** | 1 vote | 36,132,150,000 | Publicly offered in IPO; the only class available to new investors |
| **Class B** | 10 votes | 6,125,000,000 | Concentrated with insiders; not sold in IPO |
| **Class C** | 0 votes | 10,000,000,000 | None issued/outstanding |
| **Class D** | TBD by Board | Authorized | None issued/outstanding |

The 10:1 voting ratio is the foundational mechanism: each Class B share carries ten times the voting weight of a Class A share, while carrying identical economic rights (1:1 conversion, same dividend and liquidation rights). Public investors can only acquire Class A shares, permanently capping their per-share governance influence at one-tenth of insider shares.

---

### 2. Board-Election Mechanics: Class-Segregated Voting

The board will consist of **eight directors**, elected **annually** (no staggered/classified board), with **no cumulative voting**. The election mechanics split the board along class lines:

- **Class B Directors (5 of 8, i.e., 51% rounded up):** Elected by Class B holders **voting separately as a class**. Because Elon Musk beneficially owns ~93.6% of Class B stock, he unilaterally controls election of these five seats.
- **Common Stock Directors (3 of 8, i.e., the remaining 49%):** Elected by Class A and Class B holders **voting together as a single class**. Because Musk's combined voting power exceeds 50%, he also controls election of these three seats.

**Result:** Musk can elect **all eight directors** — five directly through his Class B majority, and three through his combined voting majority. Class A public shareholders, regardless of aggregate economic stake, cannot elect a single director.

Additional Class B protective provisions (requiring separate Class B class approval) cover:
- Removal of Elon Musk as CEO/Chairman or director
- Removal of Class B Directors (with or without cause)
- Filling Class B Director vacancies
- Charter amendments affecting Class B rights
- Certain mergers, combinations, or sales

---

### 3. Controlled-Company Status and Governance Exemptions

Because Musk will hold **greater than 50% of voting power** with respect to director elections, SpaceX qualifies as a "controlled company" under **Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas** listing rules. This status exempts the company from:

1. Majority-independent board requirement
2. Independent nominating committee requirement
3. Independent compensation committee requirement
4. Annual performance evaluations of compensation/nominating committees

**Not exempted:** The audit committee must still be composed entirely of independent directors (Rule 10A-3 / Sarbanes-Oxley).

The filing expressly warns Class A shareholders that they "will not have the same protections" as shareholders of companies subject to full Nasdaq governance requirements. Notably, the board currently has five of eight independent directors (a majority), but the company is **not required** to maintain this — it is a voluntary election, not a mandate.

---

### 4. The Wedge: Economic Ownership vs. Voting Power

The filing's beneficial ownership table quantifies the divergence for Elon Musk (pre-offering):

| Metric | Calculation | Result |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| Class A shares owned | 849,494,440 | 12.3% of Class A |
| Class B shares owned | 5,569,053,075 | 93.6% of Class B |
| **Total economic ownership** | (849,494,440 + 5,569,053,075) / (6,932,508,000 + 5,602,790,410) | **~51.2%** |
| **Combined voting power** | As reported in filing | **85.1%** |
| **Wedge (voting − economic)** | 85.1% − 51.2% | **~33.9 percentage points** |

Musk holds roughly **half the economic equity** but approximately **85% of the voting power**. Public Class A shareholders will bear a substantial share of economic risk and reward (the ~49% of equity Musk does not own) while holding essentially no ability to influence board composition, executive removal, or strategic direction.

The IPO itself **widens** this wedge: only Class A shares (1 vote each) are issued, which dilutes Musk's economic ownership proportionally but barely touches his voting power, because each new share adds only 1 vote against his existing ~55.7 billion Class B votes.

---

### 5. Conversion Mechanics and the Absence of a Time-Based Sunset

The structure has **no automatic time-based sunset**. Instead, it relies on a **transfer-based sunset**:

- **Voluntary conversion:** Each Class B share is convertible at holder option into one Class A share (1:1), anytime.
- **Automatic conversion upon "Transfer":** Any transfer (broadly defined to include sales, gifts, pledges, proxy transfers, and involuntary transfers, whether or not for value) of a Class B share — or any legal/beneficial interest therein — automatically converts it to Class A.
- **Permitted Transfers (exceptions):** Transfers among registered Class B holders, to family members, to qualifying trusts/entities, and to certain estate-planning/charitable vehicles do **not** trigger conversion.

This means the dual-class structure **persists indefinitely** so long as Class B shares remain within the permitted circle. Over time, non-permitted transfers erode the Class B count, **increasing the relative voting power of remaining Class B holders** — the structure concentrates rather than dissipates.

A critical control-shift risk is disclosed: if Musk's Class B holdings fall below a majority of outstanding Class B shares (through conversions), **another Class B holder or coalition could gain the ability to elect and remove a majority of the board** — even if Musk remains the largest economic shareholder. Board control follows the majority of outstanding Class B shares, not the majority of economic equity.

---

### 6. The Core Distinction

| Dimension | Governance Control | Economic Ownership |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Musk's stake** | ~85.1% voting power; elects all 8 directors; controls all shareholder matters | ~51.2% of total equity; bears ~half of economic risk/reward |
| **Public Class A holders** | Cannot elect any director; cannot remove Musk; limited influence on charter/M&A | Bear ~49% of economic risk/reward (post-IPO, before further dilution) |
| **Source of divergence** | 10:1 Class B voting multiplier + class-segregated board elections | 1:1 economic rights across classes |
| **Controlled-company effect** | Exempts SpaceX from independent-board, nominating, and compensation-committee requirements | Does not alter economic rights — dividends, liquidation, and conversion rights remain equal across classes |
| **Sunset** | No time-based sunset; transfer-based erosion only; control follows majority of outstanding Class B | Economic ownership unaffected by class; only diluted by new share issuance |

**The key analytical point:** Controlled-company status is a **governance** concept — it relaxes board-independence and committee-independence requirements — but it does **not** alter **economic** rights. Class A and Class B shares carry identical economic entitlements (dividends, liquidation, 1:1 conversion). The wedge between Musk's ~51% economic ownership and ~85% voting power is engineered entirely through the voting-rights multiplier and class-segregated board elections, not through any difference in economic claims. Public shareholders accept near-zero governance influence while retaining a proportional economic stake — a trade-off the filing explicitly warns about.

---

### Sources

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Dual-Class Capital Structure, Board Election Mechanics, Controlled-Company Status, and Beneficial Ownership Table"
    }
  ]
}

| Dimension | Governance Control | Economic Ownership |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Musk's stake** | ~85.1% voting power; elects all 8 directors; controls all shareholder matters | ~51.2% of total equity; bears ~half of economic risk/reward |
| **Public Class A holders** | Cannot elect any director; cannot remove Musk; limited influence on charter/M&A | Bear ~49% of economic risk/reward (post-IPO, before further dilution) |
| **Source of divergence** | 10:1 Class B voting multiplier + class-segregated board elections | 1:1 economic rights across classes |
| **Controlled-company effect** | Exempts SpaceX from independent-board, nominating, and compensation-committee requirements | Does not alter economic rights — dividends, liquidation, and conversion rights remain equal across classes |
| **Sunset** | No time-based sunset; transfer-based erosion only; control follows majority of outstanding Class B | Economic ownership unaffected by class; only diluted by new share issuance |

**The key analytical point:** Controlled-company status is a **governance** concept — it relaxes board-independence and committee-independence requirements — but it does **not** alter **economic** rights. Class A and Class B shares carry identical economic entitlements (dividends, liquidation, 1:1 conversion). The wedge between Musk's ~51% economic ownership and ~85% voting power is engineered entirely through the voting-rights multiplier and class-segregated board elections, not through any difference in economic claims. Public shareholders accept near-zero governance influence while retaining a proportional economic stake — a trade-off the filing explicitly warns about.
